title: Normal Distribution
summary: Use the bell curve and z-scores for real-world measurements.

slides.title: The bell curve
slides.content:
## Story

Heights of students, exam scores, measurement errors — many real quantities cluster around a **mean** with a symmetric bell shape.

**Normal** distribution: $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$

- $\mu$ = center (mean)
- $\sigma$ = spread (standard deviation)
- Symmetric, unimodal, tails extend forever

slides.title: Standardizing with z-scores
slides.content:
Convert any normal to **standard normal** $Z \sim N(0,1)$:

$$Z = \frac{X - \mu}{\sigma}$$

Then use the **z-table**:

$$P(X < a) = P\left(Z < \frac{a - \mu}{\sigma}\right)$$

**Key:** $P(Z < -z) = 1 - P(Z < z)$ by symmetry.

slides.title: Exam applications
slides.content:
Normal distribution appears when:

- Scores/grades are approximately normal
- Sample means (Central Limit Theorem)
- Measurement with given $\mu$ and $\sigma$

**Steps:** (1) Draw sketch (2) Standardize (3) Look up z-table (4) Use symmetry/complements

slides.title: Between two values
slides.content:
$$P(a < X < b) = P\left(\frac{a-\mu}{\sigma} < Z < \frac{b-\mu}{\sigma}\right)$$

$$= \Phi(z_b) - \Phi(z_a)$$

where $\Phi$ is the standard normal CDF from the z-table.

slides.title: Common mistakes
slides.content:
- Use **$\sigma$** in the denominator, not $\sigma^2$
- "Above 80" means $P(X > 80) = 1 - P(X < 80)$
- Shade the region on your sketch before looking up values

**Tool:** Use the built-in z-table at `/tools/z-table`

workedExamples.problem: Test scores are $N(70, 100)$ (i.e. $\mu=70$, $\sigma=10$). Find the probability a student scores above 85.

steps.title: Probability
steps.content:
$$P(-1 < Z < 1) = 0.8413 - 0.1587 = 0.6826$$

About 68% within one standard deviation.
